data.title: Real Estate Is Vulnerable Going Into 2026
data.description:
Regular readers of mine will know that I've been cataloging the 2025 real estate market, particularly commercial ("CRE") like it's my obsession or something. They will also likely remember this chart, which captivated my attention back in September when I wrote, "13 Charts That Explain The Current Real Estate Market."

The decline of office construction wasn't sudden, but it was quick. This chart has been burned into my mind, as it perfectly encapsulates the trend I'm seeing; not only are office workers having their roles shifted to AI, but their office demand has shifted to data center demand.

But the whole real estate market is in a weird spot, and I thought it prudent to cover where we are as we head into year's end.

CRE Is Still Really Weak

Commercial real estate has been roiling for some time, and it's looking like the recovery won't be quick, but it's coming. With the Fed lowering interest rates, we should expect to see more recovery, but that is only part of the picture. Demand just isn't what it used to be, and that's very well reflected in pricing; the index price is still below its pre-2020 levels.

CRE is still down from its '22 peaks, and recent growth is showing that it will take a long time in the current economic environment to recover. Of course, a sudden return to zero interest rate policy ("ZIRP"), which is what fueled the surge in 2021-2022 in the first place, could disrupt this. But without that kind of intervention, which I find unlikely, the index is chugging along sluggishly.

Year-to-date, the commercial REIT index has underperformed the broad REIT index fairly dramatically, clocking in a loss of nearly 9% compared to the broad index's 3% gain.

This may spell opportunity for some, but it's unclear to me what the catalyst for recovery will be, especially with the political buzz around turning office buildings into residential complexes in urban centers. This could take some commercial off the market, making the rest more valuable, but it's unclear to me what the effects of this transition will be just yet.

What is clear is that not all CRE is created equal. While I've mostly focused on office buildings, that's just because they're the hardest hit. We've also seen malls take big hits in the last few years and stay at lower levels alongside offices.

What's actually done well are industrial CRE (warehouses, factories, etc.), manufactured homes, and self-storage. These categories have all gained between 40-50% since 2018, although they are all still below their '22 highs as well.

This split isn't likely to correct anytime soon from my view, as the structural and cultural changes that brought on the steep declines in these beat-down sectors like offices and malls are unlikely to reverse. They may pick up in certain rationalities, especially those that are attracting talent and building more jobs (which often require more CRE), like in the Northeast and South. Any recovery we do see is likely to remain regional.

Residential Real Estate Is In a Weird Spot

The story is much more complex for residential real estate, namely because of the market distortions created by the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage -- this is something I detailed in my September article, "The National Housing Emergency," but is easily seen through this chart, which shows US housing prices against western economies where adjustable-rate mortgages ("ARM") and 15-20yr timelines are more common.

Prices are buoyed for residential by the droves of unmotivated sellers. I get it -- if your mortgage is lower than current rates, why move? Even if you break even on the house-to-house move, your monthly payments will skyrocket if you go from a 3% to 6% mortgage.

And mortgage rates are really sticky. Despite Fed cuts, the long end of the curve has risen over the last year, and that has meant mortgage rates are staying elevated too; they tend to track the 10yr UST rate.

While there has been some downward pressure in the last few months, rates still haven't broken below 6%, a level that has seemed to act as resistance in the chart in the past, like in late 2024 when we saw a similar fall.

To deal with this decreased demand (especially from first-time homebuyers), home builders have been offering steeper and steeper incentives all year. Firms like Lennar (LEN) are now offering incentives at levels we haven't seen since 2009. They can't offload their inventories, so they have to make them more attractive to homebuyers.

The incentives are so high across the board, not just at Lennar but also at KB Homes (KBH) and PulteGroup (PHM) too. It's gotten so bad that we're seeing new construction premiums effectively hit zero. New homes are essentially the same cost as older homes currently.

That's not a sign of a healthy housing market.

Residential Buyers Are In Trouble

While there was a surge of new 90-day delinquencies in residential mortgages this year, it seems to have already peaked. The chart below shows the total amount at the top left and then the reasons given by delinquent homeowners in the other charts. Interestingly enough, and I can't easily explain this, the only category still growing into the end of the year is "no contact," with a surge up to 20k mortgages now ghosting their lender.

The folks buying new homes are getting scarcer. The median age of a homebuyer in 2025 is 59, with the median age for first-time homebuyers now at 40.

I was serious when I wrote that the US has built "An Economy Not Built For The Young." Compound this with divergent data on children; we're seeing a record low of young children (under-18s) living at home, while we're moving toward a record high for adult children moving back in with their parents.

None of that makes me very bullish on residential home purchases, especially if mortgage rates stay where they are. I expect that so long as rates stay where they are, the group of unmotivated sellers will continue to hold onto their inventory, which in turn will keep prices up. A slower market for residential is already upon us and will likely continue into next year.

Institutional Buyers Are Coming Back

One positive note for real estate is that institutions, "smart money," are re-entering the space as net buyers in 2025, breaking the net selling trends from 2023-2024. This reversal may be temporary, especially since it is so small, but could also be a signal for more buying to come next year.

To me, it looks like smart money is calling the bottom for real estate and coming back into the space. Maybe they believe the shifts from work-from-home and return-to-office, etc., have finally fully played out, and they're willing to pick from the discount bin.

This could be a catalyst for commercial REITs, especially those that have been beaten down over the last few years. Now, they are able to use whatever cash they have managed to keep or raise through sales in the last few years to deploy into new opportunities. Net buying is a positive for the market, but without the same depth as we saw pre-2020, I am still cautious about relying on this as any kind of surefire signal.

Conclusion

The real estate market is in a tenuous position. Commercial real estate is still largely discounted from its old highs, and commercial buyers are re-entering the space, although the divergence between CRE categories continues with little end in sight for the pain to offices.

Residential homes are likely to retain their prices moving forward, largely motivated by structural factors like the "lock-in effect," which has created droves of unmotivated sellers who stand to lose by moving into a new mortgage due to higher rates. I believe that will be the case for next year, despite the pain we're seeing among residential homeowners as the median age grows and more adult children move back in with them.

The most major takeaway for prospective homeowners is to consider a new construction, as incentives are currently so high that it may be a similar price to an older home. Consider that refinancing into a lower rate in the future is possible, but by that time, the premium on new constructions is likely to move back to more normal levels.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: This Hasn't Happened Since 2018, And Could Redefine The Course Of The Brazilian Elections
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my hold recommendation on ETFs that track the main Brazilian indices. This article is the continuation of my initial coverage thesis published on March 27, 2024, and my last article on the topic was published on July 11, 2025.

In recent days, major events have occurred that could change the course of the Brazilian presidential elections in 2026, and consequently the performance of Brazilian assets. My intention is to address these themes and connect them with my investment thesis.

Brazilian Assets

Without a doubt, the main ETF that tracks Brazilian assets is the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ), which has 0.59% expense ratio and $6 billion in AUM. However, there are other options with more attractive expense ratio, such as Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (FLBR). As we can see below, the performance of these assets has been interesting this year, but is this due to a structural movement towards improving Brazilian macroeconomic conditions? I will address this next.

The EWZ ETF tracks the performance of the MSCI Brazil 25-50 Index. As a reference, the MSCI Brazil 25-50 Index presents a performance of 47.81% year to date, while the EWZ presents a performance of 47.60% according to the Seeking Alpha, so the tracking error is very low.

The ETF has 56 holdings, as we can see below. The most relevant company is Nu Holdings, Ltd. (NU), followed by Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) and Vale S.A. (VALE). The discussion of this article is very relevant because Petrobras has the Brazilian Government as its main shareholder, while Vale and other companies such as AXIA Energia SA (AXIA) have the presence of Brazilian Government nominees on their boards of directors. That said, political developments have a great influence on this ETF, especially on Petrobras shares.

Context

On July 11, 2025 I wrote about the 50% tariffs on Brazilian products, and what investors should do with their Brazilian assets at that time. In general, I recommended that investors not make any decisions based on tariffs, but focus on the fundamentals of the investment, and I consider the recommendation to be correct.

Since then, many tariffs have been repealed and the ETF's performance has been interesting, but I credit this to the international scenario, and not to the Brazilian domestic scenario, and I will support this opinion with arguments in this article.

Deep Analysis

Recently, I wrote an article about the change in the Trump administration's behavior towards the Brazilian Government, and I related this to the importance of Brazilian rare earths. The event mentioned was relevant in my opinion. However, we had another major event occur in Brazil, and my intention is to leave investors well-informed to make the best decision.

Recently, former president Jair Bolsonaro was sentenced to 27 years and imprisoned for a coup d'état, and Brazilian justice made him ineligible. Therefore, there were great expectations who he would support in the 2026 presidential elections. Many believed that the former Brazilian president would support the current governor of the state of São Paulo, Tarcísio de Freitas. Tarcísio is a candidate who pleases voters on the left and right, and has a good reputation as Governor of São Paulo, the largest state in Brazil.

Although President Lula makes gaffes several times, it is undeniable that he is extremely competitive if he is to contest his fourth election. In this sense, Bolsonaro's support is extremely important, as he could indeed decide the next election, and his decision took everyone by surprise.

The Candidate Supported By Jair Bolsonaro

Former President Jair Bolsonaro chose to support his eldest son, Senator Flávio Bolsonaro, who apparently will be a candidate for President of the Republic in the next elections. Although Senator Flávio Bolsonaro has a very political profile, the Brazilian financial market did not like this decision and shares fell sharply in recent days due to this choice, to the detriment of Tarcísio Freitas.

This is a bold strategy in my opinion, and I explain why. As I said, former President Bolsonaro is ineligible for public office such as the Presidency of the Republic, therefore, Bolsonaro must hope that his chosen candidate wins the presidential elections.

If the candidate chosen by Bolsonaro does not win the elections, there is a great chance that he (Bolsonaro) will remain in prison for a long time, given that the presidency of the republic is dominated by the opposition (Workers' Party) and the majority of nominees to the Supreme Court are also nominated by current president Lula. But this strategy can also work.

We must remember that when Lula was arrested in 2018 as part of the Car Wash operation, several left-wing candidates tried to persuade him to support a possible candidate in the elections, and Lula did not do so. He chose to protect his political capital, and when he had the opportunity, he returned to the game and won the elections. It seems to me that Bolsonaro is trying exactly the same, not to transfer his political capital to a third party, but to keep it in his family.

Reflection On The Financial Market

The fact is that the stock market did not fall just because of Flávio Bolsonaro's choice, but because of fear of another four years of Lula's presidency. You may be surprised by this statement of mine, and point out that this makes no sense because the EWZ, which tracks the main Brazilian assets, is rising strongly this year. However, I refute this thesis since the Fed's interest rate cut has boosted assets around the world, and this is the main driver in my opinion.

This is why I remain convinced of my recommendation to hold the EWZ ETF. After all, we are facing an improvement driven by international factors. But when we look at Brazil, we see that it is a country with extremely high-interest rates, a GDP that shows low growth compared to other economies, and what's worse, it is a country that has little room for increasing federal revenue. Therefore, I see little evidence that Brazil will present a great performance if current economic policies are maintained for another four years.

Another factor that makes me pessimistic about Brazilian assets is that, in the last 3 years, the average P/E was 10x earnings, and we are already at 11x profits with the recent price spike. Therefore, the risk-return ratio is no longer as attractive, which corroborates my recommendation to hold assets that track the main Brazilian indices.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

After all this construction of ideas, there is no doubt that the main risk to the thesis is the success of this movement by the Bolsonaro family. The most curious thing is that perhaps this has started to occur. I say this because the Brazilian Chamber of Deputies approved the dosimetry bill that reduces the sentence for those convicted of a coup d'état. In the case of Jair Bolsonaro, the sentence would drop from 27 years to just 2 years. Of course, the project still needs to be approved by the Senate.

Therefore, the appointment of Flávio Bolsonaro shows that the Bolsonaro family still has relevant bargaining power over Brazilian politics. We can imagine a scenario where Flávio Bolsonaro makes an alliance with Tarcísio de Freitas, and in this case, there is a real chance that President Lula will be defeated.

This would make Brazilian assets soar, and I'm not saying that based on my political opinions, after all that's beside the point. But the behavior of Brazilian assets after the announcement that Jair Bolsonaro would support his son indicates this.

The Bottom Line

Investment in Brazilian assets has never been known for stability, but rather for instability, and is no different in the 2026 perspectives. The financial market projected a presidential dispute between President Lula and candidate Tarcísio de Freitas, with the support of Bolsonaro.

However, we have reached the end of 2025 and former president Bolsonaro has indicated that he will support his eldest son for the presidential race, Senator Flávio Bolsonaro. This completely changes the political landscape in 2026.

Although this bold bet could be successful, it is a great risk to hand the elections back to President Lula and, consequently, maintain many of the economic measures that, in my opinion as an investment analyst, will not make progress in improving the performance of Brazilian assets in the future.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to hold assets that track the main Brazilian indices. In my opinion, despite the increase this year, the risk-return relationship for long-term investment in these assets is not yet attractive.

data.title: Rhythm: PWS Signal, HO Speed Bump, And The Case For A Rare-Obesity Blockbuster
data.description:
Overview

My last analysis on Rhythm Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RYTM) was in July. This is a stock I’ve been bullish on after a change of heart.

Rhythm has transformed into a unique obesity treatment play. However, instead of addressing obesity in the general population, which is rapidly becoming a crowded space, Rhythm is targeting rare obesity disorders like POMC, PCSK1, and LEPR deficiencies. On that front, IMCIVREE, a daily subcutaneous MC4R agonist, is the first FDA-approved treatment. In this basket of indications, IMCIVREE is generating ~$200 million in annualized net product revenue ($51.3 million in Q3).

My last two analyses have focused on Rhythm’s push into hypothalamic obesity, or HO, which would likely represent its first clean blockbuster opportunity. In HO, Rhythm is evaluating both setmelanotide (IMCIVREE) and an oral MC4R agonist, bivamelagon. In July, I covered Phase 2 bivamelagon data in HO, noting that its efficacy appears in line with IMCIVREE. This was critical because bivamelagon, beyond more convenient administration for patients, extends Rhythm’s “monopoly” in rare obesity.

The company has since reported Phase 2 data on another rare obesity disorder, Prader-Willi Syndrome (PWS). Moreover, there have been regulatory developments surrounding IMCIVREE in HO.

The article that follows reevaluates Rhythm in light of these recent developments.

PWS: Early Setmelanotide Signal

Let’s first dive into PWS data.

This was a single-site, open-label Phase 2 study in 18 PWS patients with obesity (mean BMI 39.1 kg/m2). Daily setmelanotide was titrated up to 5 mg for 52 weeks. The primary outcome measured safety/tolerability, while secondary outcomes focused on efficacy (e.g., BMI, hyperphagia, body composition).

As of this update (data cutoff November 14), 8 patients have reached Month 3; 5 patients have reached Month 6; and 17/18 remain on therapy.

At Month 3, 6/8 patients saw BMI reductions, and 2/8 saw BMI increases.

Of course, in PWS, these patients will typically gain weight over time. So, the fact that 6/8 lost weight is encouraging.

Another key outcome in PWS is hyperphagia. This is actually an important feature because PWS-care burden is primarily driven by food-seeking behavior and hyperphagia-related aggression/anxiety. In fact, it’s so bad that some PWS tragically die from hyperphagia-related events (e.g., choking). Regulators recognize this, and Soleno Therapeutics’ (SLNO) VYKAT XR is approved for this very reason in PWS.

On this front, 6/7 setmelanotide-treated patients had meaningful HQ-CT reductions at Month 3.

Safety was consistent with what is already known of IMCIVREE (e.g., hyperpigmentation, injection-site reactions, and so on).

Interestingly, and this is something that is being highlighted more today than yesterday, setmelanotide didn’t just reduce weight, but it resulted in “quality” weight loss. Among four patients with Month-6 DEXA scans, three had sizeable fat-mass reductions with minimal lean-mass loss (roughly 0-1%). One gained fat (+3.2%), while lean mass remained flat. Bear in mind that in PWS, hypotonia and sarcopenia are problematic, so avoiding lean-mass loss is clinically important. This is especially relevant because we know that GLP-1s and low-calorie diets often reduce both fat and lean mass.

The big kicker hiding behind all this encouraging data is its limitations. This is a tiny, single-center, open-label trial in just 18 total patients. Moreover, the binary-looking responder pattern could dictate a “precision-PWS” strategy, effectively reducing TAM. That is, if this pattern continues, Rhythm may be forced to find out where setmelanotide works best in PWS (what subpopulation).

That said, there’s also reason to believe this is a real signal. IMCIVREE is, after all, already approved for several rare obesity indications. We know that it works and it is safe (so far). We also have direct preclinical work in PWS-relevant models supporting MC4R agonism (including setmelanotide) as a solid strategy.

For instance, Magel2-null mice (a core PWS gene knockout) were hyper-responsive to the melanocortin agonist MT-II. MT-II produced a larger and more sustained reduction in food intake in Magel2-null mice than in controls (Mercer et al.).

In fact, Bischof et al. tested setmelanotide itself using the same model. The authors reported that Magel2-null mice are “hypersensitive to the appetite-suppressing and metabolic effects of setmelanotide.”

Net-net, I’d assign a higher PoS for setmelanotide as a disease-modifying treatment in PWS (>50%). The PWS treatment landscape, besides VYKAT XR, is notably barren. So, this could represent a big opportunity for Rhythm once the data package is complete.

This is just my opinion, but VYKAT XR isn’t an excellent drug for PWS. It is a 50-year-plus-old molecule that was retrofitted to PWS. Any hyperphagia benefit sits alongside a known liability for fluid overload, which can be life-threatening and contributes to a discontinuation rate of 10%, and rising, in the real world (VYKAT was launched in Spring 2025).

However, because PWS is a serious condition and lacks any other treatments, VYKAT XR will see adoption. But I suspect that setmelanotide could become preferred in PWS, despite the possibility of dual treatment. But, as Rhythm’s own data suggests (although N=2 and uncontrolled), setmelanotide could also be positioned as adjunctive to DCCR-type regimens for partial responders. So far, it appears that the MC4R agonism may layer mechanistically on top of the K_ATP-channel-mediated appetite/insulin effects from VYKAT XR.

Within this context, PWS looks less like a “winner-take-most” paradigm, which may explain why SLNO stock is unfazed (trading flat as I am writing this) immediately following Rhythm’s data. Then again, its stock could have already been priced for this (-37% in the last 6M).

Hypothalamic Obesity: PDUFA Delay Looks Procedural, Not Fatal

Regarding Rhythm’s HO prospects, the PDUFA for setmelanotide was pushed back from December 2025 to March 20, 2026, due to the “FDA’s request for a new analysis from its Phase 3 pivotal trial backing the sNDA.”

Taking a closer look, the FDA simply wants more time to re-run and review the key secondary endpoint under an alternative age-imputation assumption (please refer to the call for more context). Because this was considered a “major amendment,” three months were added to the clock.

However, as this is in regard to the first key secondary (pediatric efficacy), I wouldn’t categorize this setback as a “nothing burger.” That said, neither does this look like a precursor to an obvious CRL. Moreover, Rhythm appears confident that the primary endpoint and key secondary endpoint will both hold under the FDA’s own re-analysis.

Net-net, I’d bet on the sNDA for HO to still be approved on or before March 20, with a low, but material risk of narrower pediatric labeling or new post-marketing expectations.

Financial Health

While Rhythm makes money from IMCIVREE, it is still accumulating heavy losses due to R&D and SG&A expenditures. Q3 revenues totaled $51.298 million, but were quickly offset by $5.5 million in cost of sales, $46 million in R&D, and $52.4 million in SG&A. Compared to Q3 last year, its net loss widened to $52.9 million.

Net cash used in operating activities in the first nine months of 2025 was $90.286 million.

Rhythm ended Q3 with $416.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments.

Final Words

To wrap up, I continue to view Rhythm as an attractive rare-disease obesity platform (maintain “Buy”).

Progress in HO, PWS, and bivamelagon offer meaningful upside with IMCIVREE's current indications acting as a steady floor.

Near-term performance will hinge on the HO sNDA outcome and how quickly the company can convert the new label into durable revenue growth.

In the long run, additional upside comes from de-risking PWS and transitioning a meaningful share of the franchise to its oral MC4R agonist, which appears on track.

Key risks remain regulatory outcomes, payer reception, execution in PWS, and continued cash burn, especially if timelines slip.

Given its dependence on one product/MoA, although I am bullish on Rhythm, I also recognize it only makes sense in the context of a barbell portfolio with limited exposure.

data.title: Abivax: "Strong Buy" On Obefazimod New UC Data And Maintenance Results Coming Q2 2026
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Abivax SA (ABVX) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Abivax: Positive Obefazimod Induction Data Leads To Next 44-Week Maintenance Trial Catalyst". At that time, I reported that the company had released positive results from both of its phase 3 ABTECT 8-week induction trials using its first-in-class miR-124 enhancer obefazimod [ABX464] to treat patients with moderate-to-severe active Ulcerative Colitis [UC]. Both of these trials were ABTECT-1 [Study 105] and ABTECT-2 [Study 106], and individually patients who took 50 mg of this drug were able to achieve a placebo-adjusted remission rate of 19.3% and 13.4%, respectively. At that time, I rated the company a "Strong Buy" rating and based on its recent progress, I'm going to maintain this rating. Especially, even though the stock price has traded higher by more than 90% since the last time I wrote about it. But if it has made all of these gains, then why do I believe that it is important to keep this rating? and why investors should keep an eye on this name. The reason why is because the company released two additional data items from the phase 3 ABTECT 8-week induction trials.

One item is that it was able to report a significant outcome in quality of life [QoL] for these patients across all Patient Reported Outcomes [PROs] tools utilized. This was the most recent and more detailed data from this, which is to be presented at upcoming medical meetings. Then before then, the second positive updated data item was the release of clinical remission in terms of patients with [w/AT-IR] and without prior inadequate response to advanced therapies [w/o AT-IR] for these moderate-to-severe adult UC patients. Both of these data are ideal and lend further credibility to this entire program. With that being said, there are three catalysts that could come about from this program's development of this drug. The first of which is that the company expects to release 44-week maintenance data from both of these phase 3 ABTECT studies in Q2 of 2026. Barring a positive outcome from the 44-week portion, the company expects to be able to file a New Drug Application [NDA] with the FDA and a Marketing Authorization Application [MAA] to the European Medicines Agency [EMA] in the 2nd half of 2026.

The beauty of this biotech is that it already has an expansion opportunity underway that could yield additional value. It already initiated the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study using obefazimod for the treatment of moderate-to-severe active Crohn's Disease [CD] patients with an inadequate response to or intolerance to conventional advanced therapies. I bring this program up because it is expected to release topline 12-week induction data in the 2nd half of 2026. With the continuation of the 44-week maintenance data from the phase 3 ABTECT trials using obefazimod for the treatment of UC patients, plus the ability to expand to the other large CD market indications, I believe that this stock should remain with a "Strong Buy" rating. Along with the fact that there were circulating rumors that Eli Lilly (LLY) or another big pharmaceutical company would possibly be interested in acquiring it.

Phase 3 ABTECT Program Of Obefazimod Has Added Data And Impending 44-Week Maintenance Readout

As I noted above, Abivax was able to report positive results from its phase 3 ABTECT program using its first-in-class miR-124 enhancer, obefazimod, to treat adults with moderate-to-severe Ulcerative Colitis [UC]. The stock traded much higher on the release of the news from the 8-week induction data from both the phase 3 ABTECT-1 [Study 105] and ABTECT-2 [Study 106] trials. That is, these patients who took the 50 mg dose of obefazimod ended up achieving a placebo-adjusted remission rate of 19.3% and 13.4%, respectively. With that being said, this company is well equipped to continue evaluating obefazimod for these patients. Ulcerative Colitis [UC] is characterized as the formation of inflammation or ulcers in your colon. What makes it even worse is that symptoms come and go. By this I mean there are instances where a host of symptoms pop up continuously that a patient experiences, then a long period after which there is remission [no symptoms present at all]. These patients experience a host of symptoms, but since this company is solely focusing only on targeting patients with moderate-to-severe UC, they can be as follows:

  • Frequent bowel movements
  • Unexplained weight loss
  • Blood in the stool
  • Nausea

The main goal of this company is to eventually move obefazimod as a first-line [Standard of Care] therapy for the treatment of these moderate-to-severe UC patients. If you combine this opportunity relating to the phase 3 ABTECT program along with what I noted above in terms of the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD trial of targeting patients with moderate-to-severe active CD, then it will be an enormous opportunity. The thing to note is that both UC and CD fit into the inflammatory bowel disorder [IBD] market. With that being said, Abivax notes that the global IBD market could reach $30 billion by 2030 [Abivax Corporate Presentation October 2025, slide #5]. This is a good opportunity, but work remains. First, you have the 44-week maintenance study from both ABTECT studies to pass in terms of going after UC patients. Then, you have the 8-week induction data from the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study targeting patients with CD. The potential is there, but the company still has to deliver on these findings.

The importance of this program is that it established positive 8-week induction data from both pooled ABTECT phase 3 studies [ABTECT-1 and ABTECT-2]. The next test comes to whether or not the patients who moved into the next trial, known as the ABTECT Maintenance Trial [ABX464-107] are going to respond for an extended period of time. This Maintenance Trial is expected to evaluate patients again at 44-weeks for the primary endpoint of clinical remission rate. The thing is that the way this study has been set up is to take only those who responded to the 8-week induction portion of the study [Out of 1,275 patients randomized into it, about 678 responded and moved to part 1 of the maintenance study] and then randomize them 1:1:1 to the following:

  • 50 mg of obefazimod
  • 25 mg of obefazimod
  • Placebo comparator

The thing about these ABTECT trials is that the company is not giving its drug for easy-to-treat patients at all. What makes me say this? Well, consider that the way it was designed is that it could recruit UC patients no matter how many prior advanced treatment failures they had [Abivax Corporate Presentation October 2025, slide #16]. The thing about obefazimod is that it might be able to do well in the market because of several items that I'm about to note below, and they are:

  • Obefazimod can be dosed orally offering convenience
  • It has favorable safety profile consistent with its mechanism of action [MOA]
  • Durable response if shown and validated in the ongoing 44-week maintenance study

Having oral dosing is just more competitive, as it is an easier route of administration. In terms of safety and tolerability, the goal is to offer it up as a differentiated novel MOA. Not only that, but consider that the initial focus is to penetrate 2nd-line and 3rd-line therapy markets for these UC patients. This is important because JAK inhibitors penetrate this space. Despite this class of drugs having Black Box label warnings and safety problems [Abivax Corporate Presentation October 2025, slide #7] they still penetrate the markets I noted above:

  • 2nd-line JAK inhibitors in 2024 - Took 28% of the market share
  • 3rd-line JAK inhibitors in 2024 - Took 42% of the market share

If all goes well, not only can obefazimod take control of the 2nd-line and 3rd-line, but it definitely has the potential to also become the SOC as well.

Okay, the most important reason of all to go over this program and why it retains a "Strong Buy" rating is because of the several catalysts it has. The first of which is that it expects to release 44-week maintenance data from its phase 3 ABTECT studies using obefazimod for the treatment of these moderate-to-severe UC patients in Q2 of 2026. Should the primary endpoint be met in a statistically significant manner with respect to clinical remission rate, then it sets up the company to have global registrations for this program. In essence, it expects to file an NDA for obefazimod to treat these patients with the FDA and then an MAA with the EMA as well in the 2nd half of 2026.

Novel Mechanism Of Action Lends Credibility To Differentiated Drug To Treat Irritable Bowel Disorders

The last item I wanted to go over is that it is not guaranteed but possible that a big pharmaceutical company could acquire it. There were circulating rumors that Eli Lilly or another company could possibly be interested in buying it, but for now these are just that rumors. Still, it does make sense because the MOA is a novel one and differentiated entirely compared to other classes of drugs to treat UC. It remains to be seen whether or not this happens, but the company is definitely on track for the time being. It is possible, maybe, that such companies may wait to first see 44-week maintenance trial data, though, before possibly pulling the trigger to acquire it. The MOA is unique, and the company can do much more with obefazimod. Consider that the main goal of it is attaching to a cap binding complex [CBC] and then splicing a single, long non-coding RNA. What's the point of this? The reason why is because then this brings about a substantial increase in floating microRNA-124 [miR-124], which then binds to mRNA and then knocks down several proteins and they are:

  • Reduced expression of proteins MCP-1/CCL2 which results in reduced macrophage activation towards the gut : reduces inflammation from cytokines of IL-6, IL-23, IL-1, TNF-ɑ 4 IL-6, IL-23, IL-1, TNF-ɑ
  • Reduced expression of STAT3 and IL-6R - results in stabilizing of Th17 and related differentiation cytokines

Phase 2b results [Abivax Corporate Presentation October 2025, slide #13] take care of showing that the expression of miR-124 is sustained out to 96-weeks. The purpose of this is to show that it can stay in these parts of the body for an extended period of time.

Another good reason to go over this biotech is because of two additional positive findings it released from its ABTECT studies. This is important because they play an important role in how a patient feels on a therapy like obefazimod. Plus, it establishes that the drug works for both patient populations with an inadequate response to advanced therapies [w/AT-IR] and without an inadequate response to advanced treatment [w/o AT-IR]. This can be shown between the pooled data across both ABTECT phase 3 studies as follows:

  • Patients given 50 mg of obefazimod with prior inadequate response to advanced therapies [w/AT-IR] - having received up to 4 prior lines of advanced therapies including that of JAK inhibitors - achieved a pooled placebo-adjusted clinical remission rate of 10% [13% treated versus 3.4% placebo (rounded towards 3.0% to get final placebo adjusted value of 10%)], p-value statistically significant p=0.0009
  • Patients given 50 mg of obefazimod without prior inadequate response to advanced therapies [w/o AT-IR] - basically treatment naive - achieved a pooled placebo-adjusted clinical remission rate of 22% [28% treated versus 5.3% placebo], p-value statistically significant p<0.0001

The point of it being able to target both sets of patients and treat UC patients across the broad spectrum means that obefazimod could potentially become a standard of care [SOC] treatment option for them. In terms of QoL, it was stated that during the 8-week induction studies, all Patient Reported Outcomes [PRO] instruments had significant nominal p-value improvements that were attained with both doses of 25 mg and 50 mg of obefazimod. "Nominal" p-values in the sense that statistical signficance has not been established but favors the drug. Such improvements observed were in terms of an improvement of Bowel Urgency [BU], Nocturnal bowel movements, and fatigue remission. These are added bonuses for a drug like obefazimod. Besides having achieved statistically significant placebo-adjusted clinical remission at 8-weeks with this drug. It is always good when a drug also helps patients in terms of improving their QoL.

Crohn's Disease Program Offers Another Shot On Goal For Obefazimod

The thing is that the MOA I described above for obefazimod is not just limited to only targeting UC patients. It is quite possible that it could also be used to treat patients with Crohn's Disease. That's because this is another IBD that might benefit from the use of this first-in-class miR-124 enhancer. With that being said, the company enrolled the first patient of the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study using this drug to treat these patients in October of 2024. The goal of this trial is to establish the safety and efficacy of obefazimod versus placebo in treating patients with moderate-to-severe active CD with an inadequate response to conventional therapies and/or advanced therapies. The thing to note is that this trial is also doing what the other one was noted to do above, that is, to evaluate this drug in two phases, which are induction and maintenance therapy to treat these patients. With that, this is to be split into three parts as follows:

  • 12-week induction phase testing
  • 40-week maintenance phase
  • 48-week extension phase

The 12-week induction phase is going to evaluate three different doses of obefazimod and they are to be as follows:

  • 50 mg
  • 25 mg
  • 12.5 mg
  • Placebo comparator

With respect to this 12-week induction phase, the primary endpoint is going to be the change from baseline in Crohn's Disease Activity Index [CDAI] at Week12. This is where a catalyst comes into play for this company in that it expects to release induction data from this phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study in the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 6-K SEC Filing for the 1st half of 2025 financial results, Abivax had $71.63 million as of June 30, 2025. This was not a lot of cash, and subsequent to the first half's end, it enacted several other financial transactions to raise cash. One major financial transaction that it enacted was to complete an underwritten public offering of 11,679,000 American Depositary Shares [ADSs], each one representing one ordinary share. This was a solid offering that was done, and the reason why I state that is because it raised approximately $700.3 million after deducting expenses. This was one of the many cash raises that it had to complete. Other financial transactions that were done to bring in cash were:

  • Conversion of 350 Heights convertible notes in July and August of 2025
  • Conversion of Kreos Portion of Tranche A convertible OCABSA

With all of the cash it had, plus the money raised through several of these transactions, it believes that it has enough to fund its operations into Q4 of 2027. For the six months ending June 30, 2025, the company burns approximately $118.56 million. For this 1st-half of 2025 period, this is broken down into $91.63 in R&D, $1.76 million in sales and marketing costs, $19.17 in G&A expenses, and an added financial loss of $8.35 million.

Risks To Business

There are several risks to consider when investing in Abivax. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive 8-week induction data that the company released from both of its phase 3 ABTECT studies using obefazimod to treat these patients with moderate-to-severe UC. The risk here is that even though this first-in-class miR-124 enhancer was shown to do well during the 8-week induction period in terms of clinical remission rate compared to placebo, there is no assurance this will be observed again in the 44-week maintenance period.

The second risk has to do with the regulatory front for obefazimod in treating patients with UC. Of course, as I noted above, it remains to be seen if the 44-week maintenance data is positive so that it could actually file its NDA and MAA regulatory applications of this drug to the FDA and EMA, respectively. The risk here is that even if it does eventually submit such applications to these agencies, there is no assurance that the submissions are going to be accepted. Even if accepted, there is no guarantee that Abivax is going to gain the right to be able to commercialize obefazimod for the treatment of these patients with moderate-to-severe UC.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity I highlighted above, which is the ongoing evaluation of the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study using obefazimod for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe CD. The way that this trial has been designed is to evaluate this drug both in a 12-week induction phase and also a 40-week maintenance phase. With that being said, the risk here is that there is no guarantee that the drug is going to be able to achieve the primary endpoint of change from baseline in Crohn's Disease Activity Index [CDAI] score either at Week 12 [induction portion] or Week 40 [maintenance portion]. The hope is that since the drug works in treating patients with UC in the induction phase, it should possibly work in CD, but there is no assurance of this.

Conclusion

Abivax has done well to continue to deliver positive results from its phase 3 ABTECT program, using both ABTECT-1 and ABTECT-2 to show that patients treated with 50 mg of obefazimod achieved a placebo-adjusted remission rate of 19.3% [ABTECT-1] and a placebo-adjusted remission rate of 13.4% [ABTECT-2], respectively. What remains now is to see whether or not the positive 8-week induction data it achieved from both of these phase 3 studies translates to positive 44-week maintenance results in Q2 of 2026.

If this is proven, then this opens the door for it to submit its NDA and MAA regulatory applications to the FDA and EMA, respectively, in the 2nd half of 2026. The beauty of this company is that it is already attempting to make headway in being able to treat another IBD patient population. This would be the evaluation of obefazimod in the phase 2b ENHANCE-CD study to target patients with moderate-to-severe CD. It is expected that data from this study could be released at some point during the 2nd half of 2026. There is an expansion opportunity as well in terms of a possible combination.

That is, there is preclinical data that has shown this drug to work well with other therapies in treating these patients with UC [Abivax Pipeline]. In particular, there have been encouraging signs in preclinical testing of combining obefazimod with VELSIPITY [etrasimod], which is an sphingosine 1-phosphate [S1P] receptor modulator approved to treat adults with moderate-to-severe UC. With the phase 3 ABTECT trials moving to the 44-week maintenance data in Q2 of 2026, plus the potential to be able to file global regulatory applications for obefazimod for the treatment of patients with UC in the 2nd half of 2026, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Netflix And Warner Bros.: Forget The Noise, Let's Reason From First Principles
data.description:
Investment thesis

On the 5th of this month, Netflix (NFLX) announced an $82.7 billion acquisition deal of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD); around 85% of the acquisition is going to be financed by cash and the other 15% by Netflix stock, and this acquisition excludes the cable networks. There has been a lot of commentary from analysts and investors here on Seeking Alpha and other social media platforms as to whether the deal will be approved, and if so, will it benefit the shareholders of both entities? To me, it's obvious that this deal is going to be value accretive for Netflix and Warner Bros., provided that it gets approved. I find the bearish sentiment on Netflix overblown and highly myopic.

In the last three months, Netflix's stock depreciated by more than 20% vs. WBD and SPY's price appreciation of 130% and 5%, respectively. The concerns investors raise are that this acquisition is going to result in a huge debt overhang and an increased share count for Netflix, leading to a reduced EPS going forward.

In my view, this is an aggressive move from Netflix that will drastically increase the value of its portfolio assets, enabling the platform to grow more subscribers. With this growth, the company will be enabled to maintain its dominant position and increase market penetration, leading to the pricing power that will circumvent any margin compression from interest expenses. On the other hand, Warner Bros. Discovery has been experiencing revenue declines, with interest expenses eating up all the operational profits. It is clear that WBD's management has failed to utilize its valuable content to grow the company. I see Netflix being the saving grace as a result; I rate NFLX a buy and WBD a hold.

Growth over Margins

My aim with this article is to let investors make an informed decision by thinking in first principles and reviewing Netflix's fundamentals, as opposed to being distracted by the noise of this bidding war. Netflix margins have always been a concern to most analysts, and this goes back to when Netflix made a decision to pivot from renting DVDs to streaming content. The concerns here were that Netflix will incur heavy costs as this business model obligates it to purchase content right from entertainment studios, which was not the case when the firm was just buying DVDs and then renting them to customers through its online platform. Rebutting the bear case, the founder, Reed Hastings, who was then Netflix's CEO, replied by saying the firm has massive control on margins but not so much on growth.

Since this response, margins improved from low single digits to 24%, and the stock price has appreciated by 37x. Meanwhile, WBD has struggled to show positive profit margins since the merger was completed.

Reed Hastings's response about the firm having more control over margins compared to growth has since stuck with me. One mistake a company like Netflix cannot afford to make is to prioritize margins over subscriber growth. For the most part, margins are an engineering problem; there are so many levers management can pull to ensure the firm's margins don't get significantly squeezed. But to make more people pay for Netflix's services is a much harder problem, so for any chance of ensuring subscriber growth and higher retention, which by this acquisition is one way the firm can do so, Netflix is obligated to play an aggressive hand and worry about margins later.

On a comparative basis, it's clear that management at NFLX is better at operating the streaming business than the management at WBD. When we look at topline performance, WBD's TTM revenue peaked at $42.05 billion in the third quarter of 2023 and has since declined by 10% to $37.87 billion. In the same period, Netflix revenue rose 33%, from $32.7 billion to $43.38 billion.

When we look at the growth factor, the Seeking Alpha quant system rated NFLX with an A- and WBD with an F. YoY, NFLX grew revenue by 15% vs. the sector median growth of 3.04% and WBD's revenue decline of 4%. NFLX grew EBITDA by 30% vs. the sector median growth of 7.21% and WBD's growth of 8.58%. Gross margins of NFLX and WBD are slightly below the 54.5% sector median at 48.09% and 44.48%, respectively. However, NFLX has net margins at 24%, and WBD has net margins at 1.28% vs. the median margins of 11.25%.

Earnings Analysis

Netflix Income Statement

Looking at the Q3 results, NFLX's revenue grew 17% YoY to $11.5 billion and missed estimates by $881,000. Cost of revenue increased by 20%, resulting in gross profits increasing 14% to $5.3 billion and landing gross margins at 46.7%. Operating expenses increased by 17%, while finance expenses decreased by 33%, leading to operating and pretax income growing 12% and 15%, respectively. The massive increase in income tax resulted in net margins growing single digits to $2.5 billion, landing profit margins at 22% and missing EPS estimates by $0.11.

Warner Bros. Income Statement

WBD's revenue decreased 6% YoY to $9 billion, missing estimates by $138.9 million. This miss was driven by revenue declines from all different segments of WBD's business. Cost of revenue declined by 12%, resulting in gross profits growing by only 1% to $4.5 billion and landing gross margins at 50%. Operating expenses decreased 7% to $3.87 billion, resulting in operating income of $661 million and EBIT margins of 6.7%. Finance expenses increased 27% to $584 million, and income tax increased 153% to $170 million, which led to a pretax income of $27 million and a net loss of $143 million.

The Q3 operational performance paints a good picture that while Netflix is growing revenues by double and maintaining thick margins, Warner Bros. Discovery is experiencing topline declines and negative earnings margins.

Netflix Balance Sheet

Netflix's balance sheet as it currently stands is healthy, but it will be drastically impacted once the acquisition gets completed. Current assets increased by 7%, largely bolstered by cash reserves increasing 25% to $9.29 billion, while current liabilities decreased by 11%. The decrease in current liabilities was largely offset by accrued liabilities increasing 39% to $3.11 billion. While current debt was fully paid off, long-term debt increased 2% to $14.46 billion. Netflix clearly doesn't have enough dry powder to fund the acquisition; therefore, it will have to raise financing through share issuance and using a credit facility. This will potentially increase Netflix debt from $14 billion to north of $80 billion, as the company has publicly stated it plans to take on $59 billion in debt. Overall, total assets increased by 5%, while total liabilities decreased by 3%, resulting in shareholder's equity increasing 14% to $26.7 billion and a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.54.

Warner Bros. Balance Sheet

WBD's balance sheet is pretty much what Netflix and Paramount are bidding for, which consists of $100.5 billion in assets and $63.2 billion in liabilities. Kindly note that this balance sheet is inclusive of cable network assets, which are not part of Netflix's acquisition deal. With shareholder's equity increased 3% to $37.31 billion, current debt decreased 95% to $139 million, and long-term debt decreased 10% to $33.38 billion, this has landed the debt-to-equity ratio at 0.9. The all-cash Paramount deal of $30 per share aims to acquire all of WBD, including the highly leveraged cable networks, while Netflix's deal expects the cable networks to be spun off as an independent entity. This way Netflix is offsetting some of the credit risk associated with WBD.

Valuation grades

Netflix has never been a cheap stock, and I think after looking at the stellar fundamentals it has, that premium valuation is well deserved. The current EV to EBITDA trades at 14.53x vs. the three-year historical median of 12.53x. The spread between these two metrics has recently narrowed, following the bearish sentiment that was built by the acquisition's announcement, presenting a good entry point for investors looking to compound their returns over the long haul. On the other hand, the spread between WBD's EBITDA multiple and its three-year median multiple is widening, not driven by fundamental improvements at all but by the arbitrage opportunity that was presented by this bidding war.

Risks

For the most part, my analysis of either company has been insulated from the recent acquisition news. This is because, whether the acquisition gets approved or not, I believe Netflix remains a high-quality company, and in fact WBD will not detract value, but it will catalyze growth and lead to more value accretion in the long run. But recent news has made the stock more volatile to the downside as the bear case is building from more analysts. Investors have also raised concerns about the $5.8 billion breakup fee Netflix intends to pay in case the deal doesn't close. This breakup fee is significantly higher than normal and would lower NFLX's cash reserves by more than half, which could force the company to raise additional debt or reduce stock buybacks to cover for the liquidity.

In case the acquisition gets completed, the stock will likely continue to fall, as the company will be issuing more shares and potentially pausing stock buybacks in order to make interest payments and deleverage the balance sheet. For the near term, I do think margins are going to be compressed, but this won't be for so long, as I believe the acquisition will give Netflix the pricing power to grow both the top and the bottom line.

Conclusion

There's a famous saying that goes something like this: "Companies are first run by engineers, then accountants, then lawyers, and then they file for bankruptcy." The Netflix that concerns itself about margins over growth is the Netflix that has passed the engineering into the accountancy phase. It puts a smile on my face to know that is yet to be the case. I believe the acquisition of WBD will bring better distribution, a better product, and a better experience for subscribers, which will later be rewarded to Netflix's shareholders. This acquisition is a win-win situation for both entities, but more so for WBD, as Netflix could utilize the asset portfolio to be more valuable and profit-generating. Given that it's not yet clear Netflix will win this deal, it's wise to remain neutral on WBD and bullish on NFLX.

data.title: Texas Roadhouse: Attractively Valued, But Beef Prices Hurt (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
After dropping 18% from the 2024 highs and significantly underperforming the market year-to-date, Texas Roadhouse, Inc. (TXRH) is finally back at valuation levels I can get behind.

However, significant commodity inflation and relative sector valuations pour water on the near-term trajectory, making it much more of a long-term play.

Texas Roadhouse Has Been Bit Too Richly Valued For It To Become Attractive

I've been covering Texas Roadhouse for a little over two years here on Seeking Alpha. Over multiple articles, I argued that Texas Roadhouse is a best-in-class operator with a robust double-digit EPS growth outlook.

However, because it's such a stable and admired company, its valuation was always a bit too over-the-top for me, which is why I maintained a Hold rating throughout the period.

As it turns out, it was the correct decision, as Texas Roadhouse shares have delivered returns that are about in line with the market average since 2023.

Even though Texas Roadhouse consistently delivered industry-leading results, it wasn't enough to provide outsized returns, precisely because the stock has priced such results already.

Texas Roadhouse Continues To Outperform The Industry In Q3

Looking at Texas Roadhouse numbers from quarter to quarter, it's hard not to see that it's in a league of its own.

The last announced third quarter earnings weren't any different. Revenue in the quarter rose 12.8% to $1.4 billion, with yet another quarter of strong 6.1% comparable sales, and a 6.1% increase in store weeks.

Texas Roadhouse ended the quarter with 806 stores, with the addition of 9 stores in the quarter and 34 stores over the past year. Looking ahead to 2026, it seems that store openings are set to accelerate, with the company planning 35 openings and franchisees planning an additional 10.

So, while the top-line story remains impeccable, it's below the revenue line where things start to get a bit more murky.

Restaurant margins in Q3 dropped to a multiyear low of 14.3%, pressured by high food & beverage costs, as well as wages, and even rent, with all line items rising sharply.

Ever since the end of 2024, Texas Roadhouse has been experiencing worse-than-expected commodity inflation. They entered 2025 expecting to see inflation of 3.5%-4.0%, and are now guiding for a 6% increase for the year. The key input there is beef prices, which have been increasing constantly.

Near-Term Earnings Growth Outlook Pressured By Beef Prices

Texas Roadhouse delivered extraordinary returns over the past decade because it was a steady double-digit EPS growth compounder. For shares to continue to work, it has to continue delivering such performance.

Top-line growth isn't going to be the issue. We discussed their opening plans for 2026, which should be enough to drive 5%+ growth next year. Comparable sales are also expected to increase according to management guidance, supported by a price increase taken early in Q4. To me, Texas Roadhouse is very likely to deliver 10%+ revenue growth next year.

The problem is, again, below the revenue line. Management currently expects commodity inflation to be as high as 7% in 2026, mainly driven by beef prices:

As everyone is aware, there is certainly significant volatility and multiple unknowns related to beef prices. With that said, we are setting our initial 2026 commodity inflation guidance at approximately 7%. At this time, we expect to be above the guidance in the first half of the year and below the guidance in the second half of the year.

Of note, their guidance embeds some relief on the back half of the year, some of which is explained by simply lapping the higher prices of this year. Meanwhile, their wage inflation is also set to be higher than CPI, at 3%-4%. They are doing what they can on the overhead front, but it'll never be enough to offset restaurant-margin pressures.

I think that the base case scenario is that margins are going to settle at Q3 levels for the near future. As I've warned in previous articles, the strong profitability of 2024 and the first half of 2025 is now becoming a headwind, as comps become tough.

This likely means we are not going to see double-digit earnings growth from TXRH at least until 2027, which means the stock is likely to continue underperforming.

Valuation Is Attractive Historically, But Sector Multiples Are Compressing

At $168 per share, Texas Roadhouse is valued at 26 times forward earnings, 25 times next year's estimates.

This is lower than the past few years, but it's in line with the company's historical range.

Meanwhile, the sector has experienced a sharp multiple compression, as a pressured consumer is leaving restaurants to eat at home.

Q3 was one of the worst quarters in terms of growth for the sector, outside of COVID, and many stocks are in sharp decline, trading at trough multiples.

As we can see, the only stock that's trading in the high end of its range is Starbucks (SBUX), and that's only because its earnings have dropped sharply. Besides that, most of the stocks in the sector are trading far below their average.

Price Target & Long-Term Horizon

While the near-term isn't exciting given what we discussed, if we look further into 2027, that's where things become much more attractive.

In my mind, it's not a matter of if, but rather a matter of when commodity inflation eases, and when it does, margins will recover.

Consensus estimates see Texas Roadhouse doing 23% growth that year, which reflects margin recovery (normalization in beef prices) and further growth.

On that estimate, shares are currently trading at 20 times earnings, which is very low relative to growth and historical levels.

My price target of $205 per share by the end of 2026 is based on a 25x multiple on 2027 estimates.

The main risk to that target is beef prices remaining persistently high, but that's why a long-term horizon is important here. I think it's safe to assume that commodity prices will normalize eventually, and if it's not in 2027, then a little later.

Conclusion

After two years of following the stock, Texas Roadhouse is finally attractively valued. While the near term is going to be challenging, it's reasonable to assume margins will recover by 2027.

I believe long-term investors will do well by starting to build their positions ahead of such recovery.

I see 20% upside by the end of 2026, and upgrade TXRH to a Buy.
